summary: Joel comments on the new FASB accounting rule requiring companies to expense stock options from reported profits starting mid-2005. He dismisses the Silicon Valley objection that stock options are too difficult to value, arguing that accounting routinely handles illiquid assets with uncertain valuations. The post is brief but takes a clear stance in favor of the new expensing requirement, positioning it as basic financial accountability rather than a controversial burden.
key_points: New accounting guidelines will force companies to deduct stock option values from reported profits starting mid-2005
Silicon Valley insiders oppose expensing stock options partly by arguing their value is too hard to determine
Joel argues that accounting for illiquid assets with uncertain values is a routine and unavoidable part of financial accounting
The fact that stock option values change over time does not exempt them from consistent accounting treatment
money_quotes: Just because the value of stock options changes over time or because it is not possible to fix exactly does not mean it shouldn't be accounted for consistently.
key_insight: The difficulty of valuing stock options is not a valid argument against expensing them, since accounting routinely handles uncertain valuations of illiquid assets.
